channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Trump Failed to Secure the Strait of Hormuz in His Iran Deal
item.description: President Trump signed an agreement that Iran said gave it control of the waterway — and global energy supplies. Now, Iran’s military is violently asserting authority.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong, Michael Crowley and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump at Joint Base Andrews on Thursday. He recently said the memorandum of understanding reached with Iran was “over.”

item.title: Toronto Police Search For Active Shooter After 2 Killed at Street Festival
item.description: The shooting disrupted a weekend Latino celebration where hundreds had gathered on Saturday evening.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: Police officers stand guard as emergency workers respond at the site of a deadly shooting at a salsa-themed street festival in Toronto on Saturday.

item.title: Inside the Canadian Lab Burning Down Buildings to Save Them
item.description: At the national fire safety facility in Ottawa, scientists and engineers are increasingly focused on the threat from the forest.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Ahmed Kashef, the director of the National Research Council of Canada’s fire safety lab in Ottawa, inside a furnace used to test how long walls can withstand fire.

item.title: How Marco Rubio Is Running Venezuela From Afar
item.description: The secretary of state effectively controls Venezuela’s finances, the distribution of its natural resources and its government. His grip on the country is a vivid manifestation of American power in the Trump era.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: After Being Delayed by Trump, a Canada-U.S. Bridge Is Set to Open
item.description: A deal ended the Trump administration’s blocking of a new bridge that will ease congestion at the busiest trade corridor between the United States and Canada.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Tyler Pager
item.category: Gordie Howe International Bridge (Detroit, Mich and Windsor, Ontario)
item.media:description: The Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, in February.

item.title: Ten Killed in Plane Crash on the Bahamas’ Largest Island
item.description: The crash occurred as the country celebrated its independence from Britain.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai

item.title: Mexico to File Criminal Complaints in U.S. Over Immigrant Deaths
item.description: The nation’s president vowed to go “beyond diplomatic notes” after a U.S. immigration agent fatally shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a Mexican man, in Texas this week.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A candlelight vigil on Wednesday in Houston, at the spot where Lorenzo Salgado Araujo was shot and killed a day earlier.

item.title: An Aging Assassin Was Caught by ICE. Will the U.S. Deport Him?
item.description: He helped plot a car bombing in Washington that killed two people in 1976. Fifty years later, old government secrets could decide his fate.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:description: Armando Fernandez Larios in 2003. For years, the U.S. government regarded Mr. Fernandez as a helpful Cold War ally of sorts, even as its relationship with Chile deteriorated.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, a Youth Baseball League Counts the Dead
item.description: Children who played for Venezuela’s youth baseball league, a symbol of national pride, have been left injured, orphaned or dead.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní, Isayen Herrera and Fabiola Ferrero

item.title: Overlooked No More: Garrincha, Brazil’s Brilliant and Broken World Cup Hero
item.description: He helped lead Brazil to two championships alongside Pelé, but his on-field brilliance was eclipsed by a tragic personal life.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: Sifting Through Venezuela Earthquakes’ Rubble, and the Ashes of a Revolution
item.description: The devastation of Jacqueline Zúñiga’s city in Venezuela follows the demise of her political project. The revolution is over, but the human ties that help her cope remain.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: Jacqueline Zúñiga, 52, between several public housing complexes that collapsed during the twin earthquake. Ms. Zúñiga’s building was damaged and she is living in a makeshift shelter.

item.title: Mexico Investigates Possible F.B.I. Role in El Mayo Arrest
item.description: Accusations that the United States lied about its involvement in the notorious drug lord’s capture renewed controversy over an issue that has strained the U.S.-Mexico relationship.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:description: The notorious drug lord Ismael “El Mayo” Zambada García, was transferred to the United States on a Beechcraft King Air plane in 2024.

item.title: As More Remains Are Found in Rubble From Venezuela Earthquakes, Horror Becomes Routine
item.description: Fearful that officials would tear down damaged buildings without accounting for any remaining bodies, Venezuelans are desperately digging for their relatives’ remains.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A priest and nuns blessing the rubble of a collapsed building in Catia La Mar on Tuesday.

item.title: Why ‘Heated Rivalry’ Received No Emmy Nominations While Connor Storrie Did
item.description: Even if voters had wanted to honor the gay hockey drama, which became a hit on HBO Max, they couldn’t have. But the Emmys did recognize one of the stars.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Connor Storrie, left, and Hudson Williams in “Heated Rivalry.” As a Canadian production, it was not eligible for Emmy nominations.

item.title: Air Canada Chooses Anko van der Werff, Who Speaks French, as CEO After Language Backlash
item.description: Michael Rousseau, who prompted widespread criticism with a largely English condolence video following a crash in March, will retire.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Anko van der Werff, who will take over as chief executive of Air Canada in the fall, in Copenhagen last month.

item.title: Recall Issued for Pedigree Dog Food Sold Despite Failing Quality Control
item.description: Mars Petcare recalled cans of dog food that might contain plastic or metal, which it said were supposed to be destroyed but ended up in stores.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: A file photo of Pedigree dog food. It does not depict the type that was recalled.

item.title: Venezuelans Criticize Own Government Response After Twin Earthquakes
item.description: Venezuelans are publicly expressing their anger over their government’s disaster response, despite the risk of imprisonment.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: People look for their relatives in the rubble of a public housing complex in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday, after twin earthquakes destroyed it.

item.title: How a Four-Generation Cuban Family Survives on $60 a Month
item.description: The U.S. oil blockade has deepened a humanitarian crisis, forcing Cubans to lean on the island’s long tradition of community solidarity to provide a cushion.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Lisette Poole González
item.media:description: Analeidis Arias Marin gets her children ready for school in Santiago de Cuba in May.

item.title: As Messi Dominates the World Cup, a War Is Waged Over Maradona’s Image
item.description: A multimillion-dollar battle for a soccer god’s brand is being waged across continents, burning investors and tearing his family apart.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Lucía Cholakian Herrera
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Portraits of Argentina’s Lionel Messi and Diego Maradona at a match in Dallas last month.

item.title: Indio Solari, Argentine Rocker Who Packed Stadiums, Dies at 77
item.description: A counterculture icon with broad appeal, he compared himself to Bob Dylan and developed an especially fervent following among working-class youth.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Indio Solari at his last public concert, in 2017. Phrases from his cryptic songs, filled with fictional characters and esoteric metaphors, found their way onto tattoos and T-shirts across Argentina.

item.title: Canada Bets on NATO Partners for Its Expanded Submarine Fleet
item.description: In selecting a joint bid from Germany and Norway, Prime Minister Mark Carney is elevating Canada’s naval power and reducing its military and economic dependency on the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Naval officers from Canada touring a South Korean KSS-III submarine in Esquimalt, British Columbia, in May.

item.title: Nancy Ward, the Cherokee Leader, Stokes Division Even 250 Years Later
item.description: In 1776, Cherokee leaders looked for ways to navigate a foreboding future. Nanyehi, a.k.a. Nancy Ward, is venerated and reviled for her choice.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:description: Becky Hobbs, a descendant of Nancy Ward, staunchly defends her ancestor’s legacy.

item.title: Sites That Report Venezuela’s Missing Rise Far From Quake Zone
item.description: Despite problems like duplicate submissions, tallies gathered by the websites have been cited by news organizations and critics of the Venezuelan government’s disaster response.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers, relatives and rescue workers search for earthquake survivors under a collapsed public housing complex in Caraballeda, Venezuela, last month, The disaster has bared accuracy problems in how websites gather information on the missing.

item.title: With $8 Billion in Venezuelan Oil Money, U.S. Gives $300 Million in Quake Aid
item.description: In 2010, there was a huge U.S. aid effort after a quake in Haiti. Today, U.S. officials say they oversee billions in Venezuelan oil revenue, but aid is less.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers and family members searched for survivors in a building in La Guaira, Venezuela, that collapsed after back-to-back earthquakes struck the country in June.
